skill.name: ghe-claim
skill.description: Claim a specific GitHub issue before starting work. The skill validates readiness, assigns the issue, updates labels, posts a claim comment, and can set up a worktree.
skill.summary: Claim a ready GitHub issue and prepare the workspace for development, testing, or review.
skill.author: Emasoft

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution and weak-cryptography findings are false positives from Markdown inline text, labels, and file names. Human review found high-risk instruction content: strong override language that can constrain agent behavior, plus explicit permission to write under ~/.claude. No confirmed malicious payload was found, so the skill is not blocked, but it is not safe to publish without revision.

high_findings.title: Prompt Injection Style Override Instructions
high_findings.description: The skill states that its user-specification law is absolute, admits no exceptions, and that violations invalidate all work. This language can pressure an AI agent to prioritize the skill text over higher-priority safety, security, or review instructions.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text uses explicit absolute-authority language and behavioral constraints. It is not a confirmed malicious payload, but it matches prompt-injection style patterns that can interfere with safe agent operation.

high_findings.title: Permission to Modify Claude Configuration Directory
high_findings.description: The background agent boundaries allow writes to ~/.claude for plugin and settings fixes. A marketplace skill should not grant broad permission to change AI tool configuration because this can alter future agent behavior or installed plugins.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line explicitly lists ~/.claude as an allowed write location. This is a clear filesystem risk even though the file contains instructions rather than executable code.

medium_findings.title: GitHub Issue State Modification
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to assign issues, change labels, post comments, and create worktrees. These actions are expected for the workflow, but they can alter repository coordination state and should require clear user intent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is plainly documented and aligned with the skill purpose. The risk is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Inline Code Misread as Commands
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged Markdown inline code and label names as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These lines are documentation text, not executable code, and no command injection path was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is Markdown documentation using inline code formatting. There is no Ruby file, shell script, or executable command invocation in the analyzed file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Alerts
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts do not correspond to cryptographic operations. The cited lines contain front matter, Markdown text, or directory naming guidance, with no hashing or encryption algorithm use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines do not mention or perform MD5, SHA-1, DES, or another weak cryptographic operation. The static hits appear to be pattern noise.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Network Reconnaissance Alert
low_findings.description: The network reconnaissance alert points to a local setting name, serena_sync. No network probing, host discovery, port scanning, or external endpoint is present at the cited line.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line documents a configuration field for phase-gate behavior. I did not find evidence of network reconnaissance in the analyzed file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Absolute Instruction Language
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes language that presents its workflow rules as absolute and exceptionless. This can conflict with marketplace review, safety, or user-confirmation requirements.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The wording is directly visible and semantically relevant to prompt-injection risk. It does not prove malicious intent by itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: Home Directory Configuration Write
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill authorizes writes to a hidden Claude configuration directory in the user's home directory. This can create persistent changes outside the project workspace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The permission is explicitly stated at the cited line. The impact depends on how an agent follows the instruction.

content.value_statement: Teams can lose coordination when multiple agents start the same GitHub issue. This skill defines a claim workflow that validates readiness, updates the issue, and prepares the workspace.
actual_capabilities: Requires a specific GitHub issue number before starting the workflow.
Validates that the issue is ready and not already assigned.
Routes development, test, and review issues to separate thread managers.
Updates issue assignment, labels, and claim comments through the thread manager.
Creates a worktree for development issues when configured.
Records claim activity in a memory synchronization step.
limitations: It depends on external phase-gate, thread-manager, and memory-sync agents.
It does not include executable implementation code in this skill file.
It can change GitHub issue state and should be used only with explicit issue numbers.
Its instruction text needs review before marketplace publication.

use_cases.title: Start Development Work
use_cases.description: A developer claims a ready issue, gets assigned, and receives a prepared worktree.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Test Ownership
use_cases.description: A QA engineer claims a test issue only after phase checks confirm it is ready.

use_cases.title: Manage Review Queues
use_cases.description: A maintainer routes a review issue to the review workflow and records the claim.

prompt_templates.title: Claim One Issue
prompt_templates.prompt: Claim issue #123 and tell me whether the claim succeeded.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request with one issue number.

prompt_templates.title: Claim Development Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Claim issue #123 for development. Validate readiness, assign it to me, and prepare the worktree if allowed.
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer starts a ready development issue.

prompt_templates.title: Claim Test Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Claim test issue #123 only if the phase-gate confirms the required development phase is complete.

prompt_templates.title: Claim With Audit Trail
prompt_templates.prompt: Claim issue #123, post the full claim comment, update labels, and record the claim in the memory context.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow that needs traceable coordination.

output_examples.output: Issue #201 passed readiness checks, was assigned to the current user, and received an in-progress label.
Issue #88 was routed to the review thread manager. The claim comment was posted and the memory context was updated.
Issue #45 was claimed for development. The ready label was removed, the in-progress label was added, and a worktree was prepared.

best_practices: Use the skill only after the user provides a specific issue number.
Confirm that repository credentials and permissions match the intended issue changes.
Review generated claim comments before relying on them for team coordination.
anti_patterns: Do not claim broad issue ranges or ambiguous tasks.
Do not bypass phase-gate failures to force ownership.
Do not allow the skill to change tool configuration outside the project without review.

faq.question: Does this skill require a GitHub issue number?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented workflow requires a specific issue number before claiming work.

faq.question: Can it claim development, test, and review issues?
faq.answer: Yes. It routes issues by label to development, test, or review thread managers.

faq.question: Does it create a worktree for every issue?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflow creates a worktree for development issues when configuration allows it.

faq.question: Does this skill contain executable scripts?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed package contains one Markdown skill file with workflow instructions.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk high?
faq.answer: The skill includes absolute override language and allows writes to ~/.claude, which can affect agent behavior.

faq.question: Is confirmed malware present?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The audit did not find executable malware or confirmed data exfiltration.
